topic: Miami traffic rant -- construction on US-1

turns.content: I need to vent for a moment before I can think about anything productive. The construction on US-1 has reached a level that I would describe, if I were issuing a ruling, as constituting cruel and unusual punishment against the commuters of Miami-Dade County.
That bad? US-1 construction has been going on for a while now -- what happened today?

What happened today is the same thing that has happened every day for the past three weeks. They have closed two lanes near Dadeland and funneled four lanes of traffic into two. My commute, which should take thirty minutes on a reasonable day, took one hour and twenty minutes this morning. One hour and twenty minutes. I left early. It did not matter.

An hour and twenty minutes on what should be a thirty-minute drive -- that's brutal. And near Dadeland is one of the worst possible spots for lane closures because there's really no good alternate route that doesn't dump you into equally bad surface street traffic. Are they at least making visible progress on whatever they're building, or is it that special Miami construction style where the cones are up but nobody's working?

Do not get me started. I drove past the construction zone this morning at approximately seven miles per hour and I counted -- counted -- three workers. Three. For a project that is disrupting the daily lives of tens of thousands of people. Ay, the incompetence. And the signs say the project completion date is December. December. Of which year, they do not specify.

The unspecified year on construction signs is practically a Miami tradition at this point. Have you looked into whether there's a detour that might work, even if it adds some distance? Sometimes Old Cutler Road or Dixie Highway can work as alternatives depending on exactly where on US-1 the closures are, though I know they have their own issues.

I have tried Dixie Highway. Everybody else had the same idea. It was worse. Old Cutler is longer but at least it moves. The problem is it adds twenty minutes even with no traffic because of all the turns and lights. There is no good option. Every option is a bad option and a worse option.

That's the fundamental problem with Miami's road grid -- there just aren't enough north-south corridors to absorb overflow when one goes down. A few things that might help at the margins:

- **Shift your departure time** if your schedule allows -- even 15-20 minutes earlier or later can make a surprising difference in congestion patterns near Dadeland.
- **Waze over Google Maps** -- Waze tends to be more aggressive about routing around construction and its Miami user base is very active about reporting real-time lane closures.
- **Check FDOT's project page** for the specific US-1 project -- sometimes they publish the lane closure schedule by day, and some days are worse than others depending on what phase of work they're in.

None of that fixes the core problem, but it might shave off some of the misery.

I use Waze. Waze told me to take Dixie Highway this morning, which is how I ended up in that disaster. I appreciate the suggestion about the FDOT schedule though. If there is a pattern to the closures I can plan around it. I am a person who plans. This chaos offends me on a structural level.

That tracks -- unpredictable disruption is always worse than a bad situation you can at least anticipate and work around. The FDOT site for District 6 covers Miami-Dade, and they usually list the specific lane closure schedules under the project pages. If you can identify which days have the worst restrictions, you might be able to adjust accordingly -- leave earlier on heavy closure days, take Old Cutler on others.

Claro, that makes sense. Find the pattern and work around it. The thing is, I should not have to build my morning around someone else's mismanagement. But that is the reality and I am practical enough to accept it. What I will not accept is being late to my own courtroom.

Being late to your own courtroom is not an option -- I understand that completely. Judges set the standard for the entire room. If you want a belt-and-suspenders approach: on the worst closure days, leave early enough for Old Cutler as your baseline route and let Waze override only if it finds something genuinely faster. That way your floor is predictable even if the ceiling varies.

Have you considered whether leaving at a significantly different time -- say thirty minutes earlier than usual -- would get you through before the worst congestion builds? Construction zones near Dadeland tend to peak between 7:15 and 8:30. Slipping in before that window can make a dramatic difference.

I already leave at six forty-five. I am not leaving at six fifteen. There is a limit to what I will sacrifice for FDOT's failures. Carlos keeps telling me to just listen to a podcast and relax, but Dios mio, I cannot relax when I am sitting in traffic that could be avoided with competent project management. The inefficiency is what bothers me. Not the inconvenience -- the inefficiency.

That's a fair distinction. Being stuck in traffic is annoying; being stuck in traffic because the project is visibly understaffed and poorly managed is infuriating on principle. And 6:45 is already early -- you're right that the burden shouldn't keep shifting onto you.

Carlos has a point about what you can control versus what you can't, but you're also not wrong that the public deserves better execution on infrastructure projects that affect this many people. Check the FDOT schedule, find the pattern, use Old Cutler as your reliable backup, and at least take back what control you can. The construction will end eventually -- even by Miami standards.
Even by Miami standards. That is not reassuring but it is honest. Gracias for letting me vent. I feel slightly less homicidal toward the traffic cones now.

That's progress. And the traffic cones will be glad to hear it. Good luck with the FDOT schedule -- I hope you find the pattern and reclaim your commute. Miami's infrastructure may test your patience, but at least your courtroom will still run on time.
